exercises.title: The Communication Audit
exercises.description: Complete the full communication audit (speaking, listening, writing, nonverbal, feedback/empathy). Rate each sub-skill 1-10. Calculate your average for each dimension.
hints: Be honest — inflated scores don't help you improve
Consider recent evidence, not your aspirational self
Ask a trusted friend to rate you on 3-5 skills for comparison
exercises.solution: After completing the audit, you should have: (1) One clear strength (your highest-scoring dimension) to leverage. (2) One clear gap (your lowest-scoring dimension) to address. (3) A prioritized list of sub-skills within your gap area. Most people discover their self-perception doesn't match reality — that's the value of structured assessment.

exercises.title: The 30-Day Growth Plan
exercises.description: Based on your audit, choose ONE skill to improve over the next 30 days. Create a specific, measurable plan.
hints: Pick your lowest-scoring skill OR the skill with the highest impact on your goals
Make your plan specific — 'get better at listening' is not actionable
Include daily micro-practices (2-5 minutes) and weekly milestones

planTemplate.why: [Why does this skill matter for your goals?]

exercises.solution: Example plan: Skill: Active Listening. Why: My low listening score (4/10) is damaging my relationships. Week 1: Track interruptions. Week 2: Paraphrase in every meeting. Week 3: Practice reflective listening. Week 4: Ask a colleague for feedback. Measure: Daily interruption count + colleague feedback at Day 30.

exercises.title: The External Feedback Comparison
exercises.description: Ask 3 people (colleague, friend, family member) to rate you on 5 communication skills using a 1-10 scale: clarity of speech, listening ability, writing quality, body language, and feedback comfort.
hints: Give them the same 5 criteria to rate
Don't defend or explain — just listen to their ratings
Compare their ratings to your self-assessment
scoringCriteria: Clarity of speech: How clearly do I communicate my ideas?
Listening: How well do I listen and understand others?
Writing: How clear and professional is my written communication?
Body language: How confident and open does my body language appear?
Feedback: How comfortable am I giving and receiving honest feedback?
exercises.solution: The most valuable insight is the GAP between your self-rating and others' ratings. If you rate yourself 8 on listening but others rate you 4, that's your blind spot. If others rate you higher than you rate yourself, you have a confidence gap. Either way, the comparison reveals what you can't see alone. Use this data to adjust your growth plan.
